column1: Study#,SL,Title,Year,Limitations & Future Works
S1,1,Finding high-quality content in social media,2008,"Limitations [N/A]

Future Work [“This remains an open question for future work” referring to the use of co-training, co-boosting, or Maximum Entropy classifiers to improve classification accuracy, which did not yield improvements in their experiments (Section 6.1 Question quality, p. 9)
column2: “In the future, we plan to more specifically explore the relationships and usage features to automatically identify malicious users” (Section 7 Conclusions, p. 10)
column3: The authors state that their “results and insights are applicable to other social media settings, and to other emerging domains centered around user contributed-content,” implying future application and validation beyond Yahoo! Answers (Section 7 Conclusions, p. 10)]
column4: S2,2,Evaluating and Predicting Answer Quality in Community QA,2010,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that “it is extremely hard to pick the best answer from a set of answers for a given question,” due to large variability in the kind of questions asked, the asker’s expectations, and the answers given, and because only one of many answers can be selected as the best, making classifier training difficult (Section 4.3 Constructing a model with logistic regression, p. 5)
column5: The paper further notes that the 13 human-assessed quality aspects are “highly correlated, but do not help us create a robust model for explaining the variability in the data, let alone predicting the best answer” (Section 4.3, p. 5)
column6: The authors also explicitly acknowledge that human assessors “were given the answers without any context (other than the question)” and lacked information about who asked or answered the question, even though such contextual information is “critical in evaluating content quality in CQA” (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 8)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that “there are several other possible features of CQA questions and answers worth exploring,” given the diversity of CQA sites and their asking, answering, and rating mechanisms, and that “future work will benefit from the unique issues presented here while evaluating and predicting content quality in CQA” (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 8)]
column2: S3,3,Selection of the Best Answer in CQA Services,2010,Limitations [“There are two limitations in this study. The first is that actual users were not available to confirm the results of our analysis,” as expert evaluators were used as proxies for askers (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 5)
column3: “The second limitation concerns the nature of the corpus used for this study. Specifically, the corpus was restricted to the computer science category,” and features such as authority, accuracy, and length may be domain dependent (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 5)]

column1: Future Work [“As a part of future research the real askers in the CQA site could be interviewed to identify factors that affect their best answers selection” (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 5)
column2: The authors state that “answers from other domains should also be analyzed in future research” to address domain dependency of features (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 5)
column3: “Scholars interested in CQA research may wish to explore the automation of content-appraisal features,” which could enable retrieval of best answers “without human intervention” (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 6)]
column4: S4,4,Automatic Identification of Best Answers in Online Enquiry Communities,2012,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that “it is therefore difficult to generalise any findings without a broad base of experimentation,” noting that different online communities have different characteristics, goals, and behaviours, which limits generalisation of the results (Section 6 Discussion and Future Work, p. 14)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that they are “developing measures of answer quality, and will test them on threads with and without best answers,” in order to ensure that no best answer is enforced when none meets a quality threshold (Section 6 Discussion and Future Work, p. 14)
column2: They further note that identifying best answers becomes more important for longer threads and suggest focusing future analysis on threads with more than one answer (Section 6 Discussion and Future Work, p. 14)]
column3: S5,5,Predictors of High-Quality Answers,2012,Limitations [“The limitations of this study are along two dimensions. First the reasons for the weak association of social features in this study may be due to the selection of objective categories for analysis, and the selection of explicit features such as users’ points,” noting that only four categories were considered and that domain choice may influence feature importance (Conclusion, p. 395)
column4: “Second it should be noted that the extraction of content appraisal features is labour intensive as their derivation is manual,” which limits scalability of the proposed framework (Conclusion, p. 395)]

column1: Future Work [“In future work, there is a need to employ more subjective categories such as beauty and travel” to better understand the role of social features (Conclusion, p. 395)
column2: “For future work the reputation of users could be better represented using link analysis” and reputation propagation methods (Discussion, p. 394)
column3: “There is a need to investigate automating the extraction of content appraisal features, and this will enable the use of the proposed framework to seamlessly identify high-quality answers with little human intervention” (Conclusion, p. 395)
column4: “Further research could be carried out to reveal the influence of these features depending on the community and the domain of use,” including extending the study to other CQA services such as Naver and AnswerBag (Conclusion, pp. 395–396)]
column5: S6,6,Exploiting User Feedback to Learn to Rank Answers in Q&A Forums: A Case Study with Stack Overflow,2013,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“As future work, we intend to study a multi-view combination method for this problem, based on a meta-learning stacking strategy. We also intend to study more reliable strategies to determine the expertise of the users and their importance for the forums” (Section 5 Conclusions, p. 10)]
column2: S7,7,Fit or Unfit: Analysis and Prediction of 'closed Questions' on Stack Overflow,2013,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S8,8,Answering questions about unanswered questions of Stack Overflow,2013,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“Identifying the quality of a question can also help in this regard, which we plan to consider in a future study,” referring to extending the work to incorporate question quality analysis to better support moderators and improve response times (Section VI Conclusion, p. 100)]
column2: S9,9,A discriminative model approach for suggesting tags automatically for stack overflow questions,2013,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that “although our tagging system shows more than 85% accuracy for specific tags (e.g., uitableview), it sometimes does not work well with general tags (e.g., java),” indicating limitations in handling broad or generic tags (Section IV Conclusion, p. 76)]

column1: Future Work [“Using a large number of good quality positive documents may help resolve this issue,” referring to improving performance for general tags (Section IV Conclusion, p. 76)
column2: “Future work includes tuning the models by manual investigation to improve accuracy and further exploring the relationship between tags and responses” (Section IV Conclusion, p. 76)]
column3: S10,10,Analyzing question quality through intersubjectivity: world views and objective assessments of questions on social question-answering,2013,Limitations [The authors explicitly note that “regardless of the small sample size, results were significant,” acknowledging that the small sample size limits the amount of variability that can be explained in the models (Section Discussion, p. 8)
column4: They further state that “for most of the question quality ratings there is still a majority of the variability within each rating left to be explained,” which is partly attributed to the subjectivity of human judgments and introduces noise into explanatory models (Section Discussion, p. 8)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that “future studies should begin by generating additional factors that might contribute to explaining the ratings,” including adding new textual features such as presence of subjective words and features indicating question type (Section Discussion, p. 8)
column2: They propose conducting “further experiments with more advanced explanatory model-based methods” to fine-tune model performance once more variability is explained (Section Discussion, p. 8)
column3: They suggest building and integrating a system that can determine question quality, display corresponding ratings, and “offer the asker reasons for why the question posed is of poor quality and offer guidelines for improvement,” followed by evaluating whether askers revise questions and receive better answers (Section Discussion, pp. 8–9)
column4: They explicitly state that “there must also be work done in order to determine the link between question and answer quality,” so that askers can be informed when their questions are likely to receive poor-quality answers and revise them accordingly (Section Conclusion, p. 9)]
column5: S11,11,From bad to good: an investigation of question quality and transformation,2013,Limitations [“However, the generalizability of our findings is limited by sample size (n=129),” noting that the small sample restricts the extent to which results can be generalized (Section Discussion, p. 4)
column6: The authors reiterate that “the small size of this sample does not allow for generalizability of these results,” explicitly acknowledging dataset size as a limitation (Section Conclusion, p. 4)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that “future work should be performed on larger data sets in order to determine whether these findings are consistent,” emphasizing the need for validation at scale (Section Discussion, p. 4)
column2: They further note that “the framework for assessment established within this study can be lent to future work,” implying extension toward automated systems that flag poor-quality questions and suggest improvements (Section Conclusion, p. 4)]
column3: S12,12,Discovering high quality answers in community question answering archives using a hierarchy of classifiers,2014,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“For future work, we plan to extend our framework to other types of user-generated-content, such as microblog, tweets, and forum postings,” and the authors conjecture that with the type-dependent classification design, the proposed framework and intrinsic text features “should be able to capture the quality-related information of various types of contents more completely and accurately” (Section 5 Conclusion and future work, p. 113)]
column2: S13,13,"A Bit of Code"": How the Stack Overflow Community Creates Quality Postings",2014,"Limitations [The authors explicitly summarize limitations in Section 6 (Limitations of the work), including: the age of the dataset, as it is “a snapshot created in August 2012,” and analyses should be updated using more recent data and changes over time (Section 6.1, p. 1433)
column3: unused attributes in the dataset, such as tags, user-specific variation, demographic information, edit counts, and comments, which were not analyzed (Section 6.1, p. 1433)
column4: scalability and performance issues, as querying across very large tables is slow and would worsen if more snapshots or attributes were added (Section 6.1, p. 1433)
column5: accuracy limitations of the code-text-ratio algorithms, since some source code may not be properly marked with <code> tags, leading to misclassification of code as text or vice versa (Section 6.2, p. 1433)
column6: limitations of the FKRE readability scoring, due to mis-identification of sentences, words, syllables, URLs, error messages, CamelCase terms, and unmarked code fragments (Section 6.3, p. 1433)
column7: and limitations of sentiment scoring, including use of a non-domain-specific sentiment lexicon and misclassification of software-engineering terms (Section 6.4, p. 1433)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly suggest future work such as updating the analyses with more recent Stack Overflow datasets and studying changes over time (Section 6.1, p. 1433)
column2: examining additional dataset attributes, including tags, user variation, reputation, demographics, edit histories, and comments (Section 6.1, p. 1433)
column3: improving scalability and performance, potentially by using document-oriented databases or search-engine technologies (Section 6.1, p. 1433)
column4: improving code-text separation, readability, and sentiment analysis techniques, including better handling of URLs, error messages, domain-specific vocabulary, and unmarked code (Sections 6.2–6.4, p. 1433)
column5: and extending insights to other highly-technical communities and technical documentation contexts, as suggested in the conclusion (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 1434)]
column6: S14,14,Understanding and Classifying the Quality of Technical Forum Questions,2014,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S15,15,Chaff from the wheat : Characterization and modeling of deleted questions on Stack Overflow,2014,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S16,16,Questioning the Question -- Addressing the Answerability of Questions in Community Question-Answering,2014,Limitations [“Our findings are not without limitations. The first limitation is the small sample size (N=5,000) with subsets n1=2,000 for Part I, and n2&3=3,000 for Parts II & III,” which is smaller than other datasets due to reliance on human evaluators (Section 6.1 Limitations, p. 1392)
column3: “The second limitation is that we chose not to incorporate non-textual features (e.g., time question was posted, asker profile information, etc.) into our model,” even though such features have been shown to influence question quality (Section 6.1 Limitations, p. 1392)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that “future work can attempt to improve on this performance, using the two-step model we have developed within this paper,” referring to pushing the ROC curve and improving accuracy and validity (Section 6.1 Limitations, p. 1392)
column2: They note that “future work may include these [non-textual] features in order to evaluate the performance of predicting question quality” (Section 6.1 Limitations, p. 1392)
column3: They further state an intention to conduct “further study in attempts to add a stage to the model that would further subdivide bad questions by predominant reasons for failure,” such as too complex or asking multiple questions (Section 6.2 Findings and implications, p. 1393)
column4: The conclusion highlights “future research, including research to further improve its performance, as well as to assess its contribution in providing assistance to askers in formulating a better quality question to receive a better quality answer” (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 1394)]
column5: S17,17,An Insight into the Unresolved Questions at Stack Overflow,2015,Limitations [“Although the dataset size is relatively small, it is carefully developed based on certain restrictions, and thus represents a larger collection,” explicitly acknowledging the relatively small dataset size as a limitation (Section V Discussion & Conclusion, p. 429)]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S18,18,Evaluating Answer Quality across Knowledge Domains: Using Textual and Non-Textual Features in Social Q&A,2015,Limitations [“This study has a few limitations in that StackExchange is one of the social Q&A sites
column3: it may not represent all other social Q&A sites,” and “the four sites that have been randomly selected may not represent all other sites of each knowledge domain,” explicitly acknowledging platform representativeness and domain coverage limitations (Section Implications and Conclusion, p. 4)]

column1: Future Work [“Future research can validate or expand this model by testing it in social Q&A sites from other knowledge domains,” explicitly suggesting cross-domain validation and extension of the model (Section Implications and Conclusion, p. 4)]
column2: S19,19,Mining Successful Answers in Stack Overflow,2015,Limitations [The authors state that “while being preliminary, these findings inspire directions for future work,” explicitly characterizing the results as preliminary, implying limitations in maturity and completeness of the study (Section V Conclusions, p. 5)
column3: They further acknowledge that “due to the presence of domain-specific lexicon, examples of false positives in negative sentiment detection emerge,” noting that sentiment analysis tools are domain-dependent and may produce unreliable results when applied outside their tested domain (Section IV Results, p. 5)]

column1: Future Work [“First, the wide variety of affective states expressed in comments recommends a more fine-grained investigation of the role of emotions in SO since different affective states might be relevant to different contexts and tasks” (Section V Conclusions, p. 5)
column2: “Besides, it would be interesting to investigate to what extent emotions and personality traits shown in questions influence the answering behavior” (Section V Conclusions, p. 5)
column3: “Finally, we underline the need for tuning state-of-the-art resources for sentiment detection by adapting them to domain-dependent use of lexicon” (Section V Conclusions, p. 5)]
column4: S20,20,Automatic assessments of code explanations: Predicting answering times on stack overflow,2015,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“There is still opportunity for investigation of the applicability, adaptability and deployment of the method of this paper,” indicating the need to study practical deployment of the proposed guideline (Section VI Future Work, p. 444)
column2: “The first promising area of research would be to test the L-ROUGE algorithm on languages other than Java,” since the current study mainly focuses on Java and language contamination effects are not fully understood (Section VI Future Work, p. 444)
column3: “The second area of research one could expand upon is the correlation algorithm,” as L-ROUGE is not the only algorithm that correlates well with human scores (Section VI Future Work, p. 444)
column4: “Lastly, the actual deployment of the guideline as given by this paper should be investigated,” noting that other factors may influence answering time and false negatives could lead to negative effects (Section VI Future Work, p. 444)]
column5: S21,21,Quality Questions Need Quality Code: Classifying Code Fragments on Stack Overflow,2015,Limitations [“There are some threats that endanger the validity of our results,” including that question score may not represent exact quality due to reputation gaming, that many questions have a score equal to zero and thus unknown quality, that the analysis depends on askers properly marking code snippets with the <code> tag, and that non-code text (e.g., stack traces) marked as code was not excluded, which “could influence the effect of some metrics” (Section V Discussion, p. 4)
column6: The authors also note that for the edit-based quality criteria, they “were not able to uncover the original code fragment” and thus metrics are based on the latest edited version of the question (Section V Discussion, p. 4)
column7: Additionally, the study is limited to Java questions, and “an analysis of questions concerning other programming languages might need a different set of metrics” (Section V Discussion, p. 4)]
column8: S22,22,Utilizing Content Moderators to Investigate Critical Factors for Assessing the Quality of Answers on Brainly, Social Learning Q&A Platform for Students: A Pilot Study",2015,"Limitations [The authors explicitly characterize the study as a pilot study, noting that it involved “a total of 25 Brainly moderators in a US market,” which limits the sample size and representativeness of the findings (Conclusion, p. 4)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that the study “will attempt to scale up the study to a larger sample of Brainly content moderators from different markets (e.g., Russia, France, Indonesia, etc.),” to validate and generalize the findings, to investigate cultural and market differences in applying quality criteria, and to gain more insights into new criteria moderators may use when assessing answer quality (Conclusion, p. 4)]
column2: S23,23,Predicting the quality of questions on Stackoverflow,2015,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that “the terms were analyzed manually, based on human judgment,” noting that this process is “rather subjective” and suggesting that “an automated way to analyze the extracted terms would be an improvement” (Section 5 Future Research, p. 39)
column2: They further propose that “another matter for a future work is to include the part-of-speech tagging in the predicting models and to use the parts of speech as features to improve the predictive power of the models” (Section 5 Future Research, p. 39)]
column3: S24,24,Answers or no answers: Studying question answerability in Stack Overflow,2015,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that “this paper investigated the Quest-for-Answer framework only in Stack Overflow,” noting that results may not generalize to other CQA platforms (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 728)
column4: They acknowledge that “over 50% of the variability in questions’ answerability remained unexplained,” indicating limitations in explanatory power of the proposed framework (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 728)
column5: The study “dichotomized questions as either answered or unanswered, ignoring whether they were marked as accepted,” which limits the granularity of the analysis (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 728)
column6: The authors further note that “the cross-sectional nature of the dataset prevents any inference of causality,” restricting causal interpretations between question features and answerability (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 728)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that “future research could validate the framework by drawing data from multiple platforms,” including adapting metadata measures for sites such as Yahoo! Answers (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 728)
column2: They propose that “future studies might investigate nuances among accepted, answered and unanswered questions to yield richer findings” (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 728)
column3: They suggest that “future research could rely on experimental designs to examine causality” between metadata/content features and answering intentions, and to study how conspicuous versus inconspicuous features shape knowledge-sharing behavior (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 728)]
column4: S25,25,TagCombine: Recommending Tags to Contents in Software Information Sites,2015,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “we conclude this paper and mention future work,” and indicate plans to further improve tag recommendation by extending and refining the proposed approach beyond the current evaluation on four software information sites (Section 7 Conclusion and Future Work, p. 1034–1035)]
column2: S26,26,Detecting high-quality posts in community question answering sites,2015,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“Future work includes investigating the impact of the question/answer content on such correlation structure as well as its dynamics,” referring to extending the current approach—which deliberately bypasses content-based features—to study how textual content influences the observed voting correlation over time (Section 7 Conclusions, p. 81)]
column2: S27,27,Building a parsimonious model for identifying best answers using interaction history in community Q&A,2015,Limitations [“One of the largest limitations of this study was a lack of additional metadata and other features used in previous models,” noting that the model intentionally used a manageable number of features due to the exploratory nature of the study (Section Discussion – Limitations, p. 9)
column3: “Another limitation is selection of Best Answer as a metric for answer quality given the small proportion of answers selected as Best Answers within the Yahoo! Answers database,” highlighting the limitations of Best Answer as an evaluative proxy and its connection to asker dissatisfaction (Section Discussion – Limitations, p. 9)]

column1: Future Work [“Further study could be done to provide a comprehensive identification of community-based interactions within CQA and measure how they interact with textual-based features in order to derive more effective and efficient models,” suggesting expanding interaction features and studying their interplay with textual features (Section Discussion, p. 9)
column2: The authors also state that “one solution for future work can be to further experiment using human assessments,” such as MTurk-based evaluations, to validate answer quality beyond Best Answer labels (Section Discussion – Limitations, p. 9)]
column3: S28,28,Multi-Factor Duplicate Question Detection in Stack Overflow,2015,Limitations [The authors explicitly discuss threats to validity: threats to internal validity due to possible unnoticed errors in experiments and implementation despite double checking
column4: threats to external validity related to generalizability, as the study analyzes only 1,528 duplicate questions from over two million Stack Overflow questions
column5: and threats to construct validity concerning the choice of evaluation metrics (recall-rate@5, @10, @20), although the authors argue these metrics are commonly used in prior studies (Section 5.4 Threats to Validity, p. 993)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “in the future, we plan to reduce this threat further by analyzing even more duplicate questions from additional question and answer sites,” indicating plans to extend the study beyond Stack Overflow to improve generalizability (Section 5.4 Threats to Validity, p. 993)
column2: They also note that they “conclude this paper and mention future work,” referring to further improvements and applications of DupPredictor beyond the current setting (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 997)]
column3: S29,29,Synonym Suggestion for Tags on Stack Overflow,2015,Limitations [The authors explicitly note that not all synonym-creation strategies are implemented, stating that “at the time of writing, we have implemented 8 out of the 9 strategies,” and that Abbreviation/Synonym is not yet automated, which limits coverage (Section VI Discussion, p. 100)
column4: They acknowledge lower accuracy for unseen/new tags, observing that TSST performs significantly better on approved Stack Overflow synonym-pairs than on newly selected Android-related tags, particularly for ranking the first suggestion (Section VI Discussion, p. 100)
column5: They further point out limitations in ranking quality, noting that similar suggestions (e.g., singular vs. plural forms) are not always ranked adjacently, indicating room for improvement in ranking strategy (Section VI Discussion, p. 100)
column6: In the Threats to Validity subsection, they explicitly mention threats to internal validity due to manual analysis of synonym-pairs and threats to external validity arising from focusing the online survey on Android-related tags and a relatively small number of participants (Section VI Discussion – Threats to Validity, p. 101)]

column1: Future Work [“Future work is concerned with improving TSST to more accurately suggest and rank of tag synonyms, as well as to check if there exist any valid synonyms” (Section VIII Conclusion, p. 102)
column2: The authors explicitly state plans to implement the Abbreviation/Synonym strategy and to extend the Synonym-In-Word strategy to find words in words (Section VIII Conclusion, p. 102)
column3: They plan to consider transitivity of tags and the relation of source tags and target tags when suggesting synonyms (Section VIII Conclusion, p. 102)
column4: They propose improving ranking by combining strategies such as Metaphone and Similarity, and learning from existing approved synonyms to improve accuracy (Section VIII Conclusion, p. 102)
column5: They also state plans for evaluation on a larger set of Stack Overflow tags with more developers and applying TSST to other Stack Exchange Q&A sites (Section VIII Conclusion, p. 102)]
column6: S30,30,CSNIPPEX: Automated Synthesis of Compilable Code Snippets from Q&A Sites,2016,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“Compilability is only a necessary but not a sufficient condition to obtain runnable code,” and “in future work, we plan to investigate how to synthesize executable context for the compilable code, e.g., addressing the initialization of variables, generation of files, creation of database connections,” including considering “a feedback-directed technique guided by runtime exceptions” (Section 9 Conclusion and Future Work, p. 126)]
column2: S31,31,Evaluating the Quality of Educational Answers in Community Question-Answering,2016,Limitations [The authors explicitly state: “There are also limitations to our work. Our work can only detect high- and low-quality answers,” noting that the approach does not provide suggestions to improve answer quality, which would require deeper semantic understanding (Section 6 Discussion, p. 136–137)
column3: They further acknowledge that “our approach was based heavily on the community’s past interactions, which has limited applicability to a newly-formed community,” explicitly identifying limited applicability in new or cold-start communities (Section 6 Discussion, p. 137)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state: “In future work, we plan to study struggling users in the community,” aiming to understand why many answers are deleted and whether causes include lack of knowledge, arrogant attitude, or anti-social behavior (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 137)
column2: They further suggest that “detecting and helping struggle users also increase the quality of the site,” implying future work on identifying latent features of struggling users and providing support mechanisms to improve overall answer quality (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 137)]
column3: S32,32,Improving the Quality of Code Snippets in Stack Overflow,2016,Limitations [The authors explicitly note that “the code snippets recommended by ExRec are less concise,” acknowledging a trade-off between conciseness and completeness/understandability (Section 7 Conclusions and Future Work, p. 1495)
column4: The evaluation is based on “a user study with 10 programmers,” indicating a small sample size that may limit generalizability of the findings (Section 5 Evaluation, p. 1494–1495)]

column1: Future Work [“In future, we plan to improve the performance of the search operation of the ExRec,” particularly to “provide users more appropriate Q&As from Stack Overflow with respect to the context of the programming task at hand” (Section 7 Conclusions and Future Work, p. 1495)
column2: The authors further state that they “intend to improve the quality of the code snippets we recommend to a programmer to post as an answer to a selected question in Stack Overflow” (Section 7 Conclusions and Future Work, p. 1495)]
column3: S33,33,Mining Duplicate Questions in Stack Overflow,2016,Limitations [The authors explicitly discuss threats to validity: (i) the manual investigation of why duplicate questions are submitted was conducted on “a small number of questions,” because manual analysis is time consuming, which limits representativeness (Section 8 Threats to Validity, p. 410)
column4: (ii) the evaluation considers questions covering only six popular programming languages, which may limit generalizability to other languages or domains (Section 8 Threats to Validity, p. 410)
column5: (iii) DupPredictor was re-implemented due to unavailability of original data and code, and although care was taken, “we cannot guarantee that our replication … does not contain any error,” posing a threat to internal validity (Section 8 Threats to Validity, p. 410)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state: “In the future, we plan to evaluate our technique with more questions covering various other programming languages,” indicating extension beyond the current six languages (Section 9 Conclusion, p. 410)
column2: They also state that “we are currently working on implementing the technique as a web service,” pointing to future deployment and practical integration (Section 9 Conclusion, p. 410)]
column3: S34,34,Predicting Questions' Scores on Stack Overflow,2016,Limitations [The authors explicitly note that their study focuses only on “factors that are measurable based on the available dataset,” acknowledging that many other elements (e.g., users’ interests, whether a question discusses a common problem, or how well it is supported by facts, details, and examples) that may influence upvotes are not captured in their model (Section 5 Discussion, p. 6)
column4: They also state that some independent variables (views, favourites, answers, comments) “occur after the submission of the question” and change simultaneously with question scores, and thus “we cannot confirm if these variables are independent from the scores of questions,” indicating uncertainty about causal direction and independence of predictors (Section 5.1 Threats to Validity, p. 6)
column5: Additionally, they acknowledge potential limitations in sentiment analysis, as the removal of HTML tags might affect polarity and subjectivity computation, even though they observed only slight changes (Section 5.1 Threats to Validity, p. 6)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “there is a big room for improving the prediction of questions quality,” and suggest that the topics of tags may help the prediction of questions (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 7)
column2: They propose performing analysis on the writing style of the author, including grammar and spelling, as additional predictive features (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 7)
column3: They further suggest isolating high-scored questions and learning common features among them using a rule-based classifier to improve prediction models (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 7)]
column4: S35,35,Social Question Answering: Textual, User, and Network Features for Best Answer Prediction",2016,"Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S36,36,Understanding Question Quality through Affective Aspect in Q&A Site,2016,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that their study “could only extract the external features: Content Length, sentence or word count, and readability features,” and that they “cannot understand the meaning of the question content and make any recommendation of how to fix it,” highlighting a limitation in semantic understanding and actionable guidance (Section 5 Discussion, p. 15)
column3: They further note that unanswered questions are not always due to how a question is written, but sometimes because “the question itself is just ‘Too hard, too specific or too time consuming’,” which the current approach cannot address (Section 5 Discussion, p. 15)
column4: In the Threats to Validity subsection, they explicitly acknowledge external validity limitations, noting that only a sampled dataset of 38,232 questions was used due to computational constraints, and that the study was conducted only on Stack Overflow, which may limit generalizability to other Q&A communities such as Yahoo! Answers (Section 5.1 Threats to Validity, p. 15)
column5: They also state that although 31 features were used, “we may have overlooked some other features that may also improve the performance of our models,” such as question topics (Section 5.1 Threats to Validity, p. 15)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “further studies are required” to be able to completely solve the problem of unanswered questions, especially to go beyond surface-level features and understand deeper causes (Section 5 Discussion, p. 15)
column2: They state that “in our future work, we would like to look for the useful features to improve our prediction model,” explicitly mentioning the exploration of additional features such as question topics (Section 5.1 Threats to Validity, p. 15)
column3: In the conclusion, they state that “in future, we could guide or recommend them on how to edit their question,” indicating future work toward providing guidance or recommendations to help users improve question quality (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 16)]
column4: S37,37,Embedded Emotion-based Classification of Stack Overflow Questions Towards the Question Quality Prediction,2016,Limitations [The authors explicitly discuss several threats to validity: programming questions often contain items beyond natural language text (e.g., equations, emails, symbols, hyperlinks, and code segments), which “can pose as noise, and the reported results might be affected,” even though additional experiments suggest limited impact (Section V Threats to Validity, p. 6)
column5: The dataset is imbalanced (73% up-voted vs. 27% down-voted questions), and although a balanced dataset improves performance, the original imbalance means classifiers “do not perform equally for both types of questions” (Section V Threats to Validity, p. 6)
column6: The study assumes that positive-scored questions are high quality and negative-scored questions are low quality, relying on crowd-sourced votes as ground truth, and the authors explicitly state that they “cannot guarantee that all positive-scored questions contain high quality content” (Section V Threats to Validity, p. 6)]

column1: Future Work [“In future, we plan to explore the scope of sentiment aspects in various classification, filtration and management of StackOverflow questions,” explicitly indicating extension of sentiment-based features beyond the current quality prediction task (Section VI Conclusion and Future Work, p. 7)]
column2: S38,38,It Takes Two to Tango: Deleted Stack Overflow Question Prediction with Text and Meta Features,2016,Limitations [The authors explicitly discuss several threats to validity: programming questions often contain items beyond natural language text (e.g., equations, emails, symbols, hyperlinks, and code segments), which “can pose as noise, and the reported results might be affected,” even though additional experiments suggest limited impact (Section V Threats to Validity, p. 6)
column3: The dataset is imbalanced (73% up-voted vs. 27% down-voted questions), and although a balanced dataset improves performance, the original imbalance means classifiers “do not perform equally for both types of questions” (Section V Threats to Validity, p. 6)
column4: The study assumes that positive-scored questions are high quality and negative-scored questions are low quality, relying on crowd-sourced votes as ground truth, and the authors explicitly state that they “cannot guarantee that all positive-scored questions contain high quality content” (Section V Threats to Validity, p. 6)]

column1: Future Work [“In the future, we plan to explore the scope of sentiment aspects in various classification, filtration, and management of StackOverflow questions,” explicitly indicating extension of sentiment-based features beyond the current quality prediction task (Section VI Conclusion and Future Work, p. 7)]
column2: S39,39,By the Community & For the Community: A Deep Learning Approach to Assist Collaborative Editing in Q&A Sites,2017,Limitations [The authors explicitly note that the proposed model “considers only the local context of a sentence,” and that supporting more complex edits (e.g., adding information requiring surrounding context) would require “the broader context of the sentence (i.e., previous and subsequent sentences)” (Section 5.4.2 In which cases does our model recommend low-quality sentence edits?, p. 32:15)
column3: They further state that the RNN model “cannot encode long-distance editing patterns very well,” and that its performance “decays as the sentence length increases,” because the model may forget earlier information in long sentences (Section 5.4.2, p. 32:15)
column4: The authors also acknowledge that “different users may have different opinions regarding what should or should not be edited,” leading to revert-back edits and non-obvious editing patterns that “machine learning techniques cannot effectively encode” (Section 5.4.2, p. 32:15)
column5: Additionally, they note that predicting whether an edit is “significant enough” to be accepted is “out of scope of this work” (Section 6 Assisting Novice Post Editors in Post Editing, p. 32:16)
column6: Finally, they explicitly discuss that the study is based on Stack Overflow data, and although transferable, applicability to other communities depends on “availability of sufficient archival editing data” and differing content/editing practices (Section 8.2 Generalizability of Our Approach and Findings, p. 32:18–19)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that supporting edits requiring broader context will be addressed in the future by considering “previous and subsequent sentences” beyond sentence-level modeling (Section 5.4.2, p. 32:15)
column2: They explicitly plan to explore “assisting code formatting,” as suggested by trusted contributors (e.g., detecting poor indentation and code readability issues) (Section 7 Feedbacks of Trusted Post Editors, p. 32:17)
column3: They outline future work on “integrat[ing] our tool in Stack Overflow,” studying how it should be deployed, whether recommended edits will be accepted in practice, and how it may impact editors’ behavior and the edit-review process (Section 8.1 Impact on Social Process and Quality Control, p. 32:18
column4: Section 9 Conclusion and Future Work, p. 32:19)
column5: They also state that “future comparative studies are required to confirm the extent to which the training data size affects our approach,” especially when applying it to other online communities (Section 8.2, p. 32:19)
column6: Finally, they suggest extending the approach to other platforms such as other Stack Exchange sites, Wikipedia, and Quora, with necessary adaptations to domain-specific editing characteristics (Section 8.2 and Section 9, p. 32:19)]
column7: S40,40,Detecting Duplicate Posts in Programming QA Communities via Latent Semantics and Association Rules,2017,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that PCQADup fails in certain cases, including implicit expressions where key error terms are not explicitly mentioned, difficult paraphrases with little word overlap, and cross-language duplicates (e.g., PHP vs. jQuery), which the current approach does not handle (Section 6 Discussion and Conclusion, p. 1228)
column8: They also note that the effectiveness of topical similarity and association score features is limited on smaller datasets (e.g., CQADupStack), where there are too few duplicate posts to reliably learn topics and document embeddings (Section 4.2.5 Performance on CQADupStack, p. 1227)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state: “We leave the development of techniques that tackle cross-language duplicate detection for future work,” referring to duplicate questions spanning different programming languages (Section 6 Discussion and Conclusion, p. 1228)
column2: They further state that “the performance of association score feature and topical similarity feature can be further improved if we mine association pairs for different programming languages separately and we learn topics from all the posts instead of only duplicate and equal number of non-duplicate questions,” identifying concrete extensions to feature learning and dataset construction (Section 6 Discussion and Conclusion, p. 1228)]
column3: S41,41,Discerning the Quality of Questions in Educational Q&A using Textual Features,2017,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that “our sample is much smaller compared to the data sets used in other studies, which is a major limitation of this work,” acknowledging that the limited dataset size restricts robustness and comparative strength of the results (Section 5 Discussion, p. 331)
column4: They further note that the model “fails to classify the questions based on poor syntax and inconsistency,” as reflected by low ROC values, indicating limitations of the current textual feature set in handling grammatical errors, informal language, and special characters (Section 5 Discussion, pp. 331–332)
column5: The authors also acknowledge that the studied classes are “highly imbalanced,” which biases the classifier toward majority classes and leads to poor performance for minority categories such as “poor syntax” and “inconsistent” (Section 4 Findings, p. 331)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that they “would like to attempt to further experiment and obtain better results for the ROC curve in future research,” particularly for poorly performing classes (Section 5 Discussion, p. 331)
column2: They propose developing “better techniques for parsing the misspellings and grammatical errors present in the questions” to improve classification accuracy (Section 5 Discussion, p. 331)
column3: They state that “future studies could divide the questions into categories – such as fact-based, opinion-based, and advisory,” to build more specialized classification models (Section 5 Discussion, p. 332)
column4: They suggest that “future work may also include decision trees to individually detect the issues present in a particular class-label” and the use of SMOTEBoost/Adaboost to address class imbalance (Section 5 Discussion, p. 332)
column5: They further note that it would be “interesting to see in future research how well the classifier performs in other items for unanswered questions,” and whether improving question quality also improves answer quality (Section 5 Discussion, p. 332)
column6: In the conclusion, they emphasize future research toward “wider question reformulation and revision,” including frameworks that help users reformulate poor questions to receive better answers (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 332)]
column7: S42,42,TAGme: A Topical Folksonomy Based Collaborative Filtering for Tag Recommendation in Community Sites,2017,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“As future work, we will perform experiments and expand TAGme algorithm on different areas and datasets,” indicating extension beyond the current Stack Overflow and StackExchange datasets (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 5)
column2: “Furthermore, for reducing the time of collaborative filtering of TAGme model, parallel computing frameworks will be implemented such as MapReduce for the faster tag recommendation process,” explicitly proposing scalability and performance improvements via parallelization (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 5)]
column3: S43,43,Identifying Textual Features of High-Quality Questions: An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow,2017,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that “we only consider the dataset from Stack Overflow,” and note that “it is unclear whether the same findings suitable for all Q&A websites,” indicating limited generalizability beyond Stack Overflow (Section IV Threats to Validity, p. 640)
column4: They acknowledge that using voting score as the indicator of question quality has limitations, since “some questions attract high scores simply because their topics are interesting,” and that voting score “may not be able to represent exact quality” (Section IV Threats to Validity, p. 640)
column5: They explicitly note that they may have overlooked some representative factors, as textual features were selected based on intuition, which could limit completeness of the analysis (Section IV Threats to Validity, p. 640)
column6: They also state a limitation in the use of SentiStrength for sentiment analysis, noting that it was designed for short informal social text and “might not be applicable for domains like software engineering” (Section IV Threats to Validity, p. 640)]

column1: Future Work [The authors state that “further research is necessary to verify the generalization of our conclusions on other communities (e.g., Yahoo! Answers)” (Section IV Threats to Validity, p. 640)
column2: They state that they “intend to incorporate controls of these influential factors (e.g., topic and user popularity) in our future work,” to mitigate limitations of voting score as a quality proxy (Section IV Threats to Validity, p. 640)
column3: They note that “further study is required to explore other metrics such as question utility” beyond voting score (Section IV Threats to Validity, p. 640)
column4: In the conclusion, they explicitly state that their work “is continuing to investigate additional features that can potentially affect the question quality,” and that they plan “the development of a prototype tool … for quantitatively assessing the quality of a certain question and providing targeted suggestions on what should be done to make the question better” (Section VI Conclusions and Future Work, p. 641)]
column5: S44,44,Scalable tag recommendation for software information sites,2017,Limitations [The authors explicitly discuss limitations under Section VI: Threats to Validity. They note that the approach assumes existing tags in software information sites are correct, but “human errors are inevitable,” and issues such as a large number of synonymous tags “cannot be completely solved” (Section VI.1 Potentially Biased Results, p. 280)
column6: They acknowledge generalizability limitations, stating that although TagMulRec is evaluated on five software information sites, “more case studies are needed to generalize our findings” (Section VI.2 Generalizability of Algorithms, p. 280)
column7: They also mention limitations of evaluation metrics, noting that Recall@k, Precision@k, F1-score@k, and time usage may not be fully suitable due to differences in environments, and that better metrics might exist (Section VI.3 Suitability of Evaluation Metrics, p. 280)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “our current work is based on text only,” and that in the future, we plan to consider code snippets to make our tag recommendation more accurate (Section VII Conclusion and Future Work, p. 281)
column2: They also state that they “will also conduct experiments on more software information sites with more evaluation metrics,” indicating plans for broader empirical validation and richer evaluation (Section VII Conclusion and Future Work, p. 281)
column3: Additionally, they note that multi-label classification evaluation metrics will be explored in future work to better suit the tag recommendation task (Section VI.3 Suitability of Evaluation Metrics, p. 280)]
column4: S45,45,Towards Question Improvement on Knowledge Sharing Platforms: A Stack Overflow Case Study,2017,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S46,46,Question's advisor a wizard interface to teach novice programmers how to post ""better"" questions in stack overflow",2017,"Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“For future work, we aim to test our solution locally, collecting feedback, and reporting what happened” (Section 6 Conclusions and Future Work, p. 478)
column2: “In addition, we need to do a further investigation on how the presence of these features relates to question’s performance” (Section 6 Conclusions and Future Work, p. 478)
column3: “This research would also have the goal of finding new relevant characteristics that were not suggested through the questionnaire” (Section 6 Conclusions and Future Work, p. 478)
column4: “Last, we want to design a plugin that allows people to add our suggestion engine in any text area component,” enabling integration into platforms such as Moodle or Facebook (Section 6 Conclusions and Future Work, p. 478)]
column5: S47,47,On early detection of high voted Q&A on Stack Overflow,2017,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“In our next steps, we tend to extend our model to take into consideration the users behavior in high-quality content detection problem,” indicating incorporation of user-related features beyond question–answer interactions (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 797)
column2: “In addition, we are planning to investigate the effect of high-quality content prediction on the performance of expert finding and expert profiling tasks on Stack Overflow,” suggesting downstream applications of the proposed model (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 797)]
column3: S48,48,The best answer prediction by exploiting heterogeneous data on software development Q&A forum,2017,Limitations [The authors explicitly note that “Stack Overflow is just one of many Q&A websites,” and that “the pattern we find out in Stack Overflow might be migrated to the other websites,” implicitly acknowledging limited generalizability, as the empirical study is conducted only on Stack Overflow data (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 218–219)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “if there exists some interesting patterns in other websites, we want to leverage them, which can be deferred to the future work,” indicating plans to extend and validate the proposed BoostStack framework on other Q&A websites beyond Stack Overflow (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 219)]
column2: S49,49,Language Use Matters: Analysis of the Linguistic Structure of Question Texts Can Characterize Answerability in Quora,2017,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S50,50,Understanding Stack Overflow Code Fragments,2017,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [“As next steps in this research project, we plan to investigate which of the identified code fragment issues have the most impact on how self-explanatory a code fragment is,” and to study “to what extent the identified issues are resolved by the original text that accompanies each code fragment,” as well as “how to identify the most useful explanation in the text” (Section VI Future Work, p. 5)
column2: “Another promising avenue for future work is the automated classification of code fragments based on how self-explanatory they are” (Section VI Future Work, p. 5)
column3: The authors further state that “the ultimate goal of this work is understanding developers’ information needs related to code fragments and how these needs can be addressed by documentation guidelines or on-demand developer documentation” (Section VI Future Work, p. 5)]
column4: S51,51,Are Code Examples on an Online Q&A Forum Reliable? A Study of API Misuse on Stack Overflow,2018,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that “our study is limited to 100 Java APIs that frequently appear in Stack Overflow and thus may not generalize to other Java APIs or different languages,” acknowledging limited API and language coverage (Section Threats to Validity, p. 10)
column5: They note that the scope is limited to code snippets found on Stack Overflow, and that “other types of online resources such as programming blogs and other Q&A forums may have better curated examples,” limiting external validity (Section Threats to Validity, p. 10)
column6: They explicitly acknowledge that ExampleCheck detects API misuse with 72% precision, and that false positives exist because the approach relies on sequence comparison “without deep knowledge of its specification,” indicating methodological limitations and imperfect precision (Sections 4.2 and Threats to Validity, pp. 6, 10)
column7: They also note that infrequent but correct alternative API usage patterns may not be learned from GitHub due to low support, leading to false positives (Section 4.2, p. 7)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state: “As a future work, we plan to validate ExampleCheck with developers and solicit their feedback on its Chrome extension,” indicating plans for user studies and empirical validation of the tool in practice (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 10)]
column2: S52,52,Data-Driven Proactive Policy Assurance of Post Quality in Community Q&A Sites,2018,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that their approach focuses only on four types of medium-level post edits (code formatting, text formatting, hyperlink modification, and image revision), and that major text or code changes are much more complicated and require deeper understanding, which is beyond the scope of the current work (Section 3.3 What is the scale of changes that post edits involve?, p. 8)
column3: They also explicitly note that their CNN-based approach cannot perfectly model non-obvious or conflicting editing patterns, such as revert-back edits caused by differing user opinions, and that machine-learning-based recommendations are not perfect and may produce erroneous or missed recommendations (Section 6.1 The Impact on Collaborative Editing, pp. 18–19)
column4: Additionally, they acknowledge that the effectiveness of the approach depends on the availability of sufficient historical collaborative editing data, which may limit applicability to communities with sparse edit histories (Section 6.2 The Generalization of Our Approach and Findings, pp. 19–20)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that major post changes (e.g., adding new code examples or substantial content revisions) are left as future work, as they require deeper semantic understanding of post content and discussion context (Section 3.3, p. 8)
column2: They note that updating the model to learn newly emerging editing patterns over time is a future challenge as community norms evolve (Section 6.1, p. 18)
column3: They explicitly state that evaluating the approach in large-scale, live deployment on Stack Overflow remains an open question and requires further investigation (Section 6.2.2 The Generalization to Other Sites, p. 20)
column4: Finally, they suggest that exploring effective mechanisms to blend machine-learning-based post-edit recommendations with human collaborative editing is an important and interesting future research direction (Section 6.1, p. 19)]
column5: S53,53,Duplicate Detection in Programming Question Answering Communities,2018,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]
column2: S54,54,Two Perspectives on Software Documentation Quality in Stack Overflow,2018,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that the acceptance of an answer “might also depend on the context and current activity of the developer who asks a question (e.g., using an IDE and developing for Python or learning about a software product), which needs further investigation,” indicating future work on studying developer context and activity when assessing documentation quality and answer acceptance (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 9)]
column2: S55,55,Determinants of quality, latency, and amount of Stack Overflow answers about recent Android APIs",2018,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study focuses only on the Android ecosystem and Stack Overflow, noting that “our focus on Android alone may affect generalizability to other domains,” which limits the external validity of the findings beyond Android APIs and Stack Overflow–based Q&A communities (Related Work – Stack Overflow Q&A quality and latency, p. 5)
column3: they explicitly note that their API-linking strategy was intentionally designed to favor precision over recall, achieving high precision (96%) but relatively low recall (60%), which may cause some relevant API-related questions to be missed (Stack Overflow question API links, p. 8–9)
column4: the authors also acknowledge that their assumption underlying the answer-quality metric—that users are equally likely to up-vote answers over time—“may not be true,” posing a threat to construct validity (Question and answer quality, p. 12)
column5: additionally, they note that their operationalization of documentation need based on model residuals is “one possible explanation of many,” acknowledging potential construct validity limitations (Residual question need for documentation, p. 12–13)
column6: finally, they acknowledge that the relatively low R˛ values in the time-to-answer models increase uncertainty in estimates, even though the models remain suitable for inference (Interpreting regression results, p. 15–16)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “there is no comprehensive (e.g., qualitative) work on the social reasoning behind high Stack Overflow question scores,” and that understanding the underlying social mechanisms driving why high-quality questions often take longer to receive answers “could be the subject of future work,” indicating future research on qualitative and sociotechnical explanations of voting and answering behavior on Stack Overflow (Latency of answers to new API questions, Discussion, p. 21)]
column2: S56,56,ICSD: An Automatic System for Insecure Code Snippet Detection in Stack Overflow over Heterogeneous Information Network,2018,Limitations [The authors explicitly state that their empirical evaluation focuses on Java code snippets for Android application development as a “case study,” noting that Java and Android are selected due to their popularity and security relevance, which implicitly limits the generalizability of the results to other programming languages and platforms (Section 3.1 Feature Extraction, p. 3–4)
column3: they also rely on manually labeled ground truth for a subset of code snippets based on domain expertise and predefined vulnerability categories, which may introduce subjectivity and limit scalability to other security taxonomies (Section 4.1 Experimental Setup, p. 7)
column4: additionally, the study is conducted exclusively on Stack Overflow data, implicitly limiting external validity to other social coding platforms (Sections 4–6)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “the proposed method and developed system can also be easily expanded to code security analysis in other social coding platforms, such as GitHub and Stack Exchange,” indicating future work on extending ICSD beyond Stack Overflow to other platforms and ecosystems (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 10)]
S57,57,"EnTagRec ++: An enhanced tag recommendation system for software information sites

,2018,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that ENTAGREC++ performs less effectively on FREECODE compared to Stack Exchange sites, noting that “objects in FREECODE are much shorter,” and that the Bayesian inference component based on L-LDA “usually requires training documents to be relatively long in order to achieve good results,” which limits effectiveness for short-text software objects and poses a construct validity limitation (Section 9.3 Discussion, pp. 23–24)
column2: they also explicitly note that tags associated with fewer than 50 objects are filtered out to reduce noise, which may exclude rare or emerging tags and limit coverage of long-tail tagging scenarios (Section 9.1 Experimental Setting, p. 18)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that their approach can be further enhanced by better handling short software objects, as the discussion highlights the need for techniques beyond LDA to improve tag recommendation for short texts such as FREECODE project descriptions, indicating future work on adapting or replacing topic models for short-content scenarios (Section 9.3 Discussion, pp. 23–24)]
column2: S58,58,Exploiting User Activities for Answer Ranking in Q&A Forums,2018,Limitations [N/A]

column1: Future Work [N/A]

S59,59,Guiding supervised topic modeling for content based tag recommendation,2018,"Limitations [The authors explicitly note that, in their experimental setting, “we remove some low frequency tags,” acknowledging that this preprocessing choice may limit the evaluation and effectiveness of the proposed approach for rare or infrequently used tags, and thus constrains the coverage of the tag recommendation task (Section 5.1 Experimental setup, p. 486)
column2: additionally, the study focuses on content-based tag recommendation using textual content only, implicitly limiting applicability to scenarios where rich textual information is available and not addressing multimedia or non-textual content (Sections 1 Introduction and 5 Experimental evaluations, pp. 479–487)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state several future research directions: (1) “in the future, we consider to apply deep learning models such as convolutional neural networks and recurrent neural networks to see their effectiveness,” indicating plans to replace or augment topic modeling with deep learning approaches
column2: (2) they state that they are “interested in checking the effectiveness of the existing methods for recommending these low frequency tags,” highlighting future work on handling rare tags that were excluded in the current study
column3: and (3) they note that “the tags for the same piece of content may be correlated with each other” and that they “plan to incorporate this phenomenon in the future work,” indicating future extensions to model tag–tag correlations (Section 7 Conclusions, p. 488)]
column4: S60,60,How to ask for technical help? Evidence-based guidelines for writing questions on Stack Overflow,2018,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge several limitations of their study: (i) they note that the analysis relies on sentiment analysis using a general-purpose tool (SentiStrength), which “is not trained specifically for the software engineering domain,” leading to misclassifications, especially for questions written by expert users rich in technical jargon (Section 4.2 Qualitative analysis of emotions, p. 194–195)
column5: (ii) they acknowledge a limitation of the Stack Overflow data dump, namely that reputation scores are snapshots taken at dump time, forcing them to approximate user reputation at posting time by restricting the dataset to the last month of the dump, which may still introduce inaccuracies (Section 3 Dataset, p. 189–190)
column6: (iii) they explicitly note that edited questions had to be removed because “we cannot know how a question edited afterward looked like when the accepted answer was provided,” which may limit the representativeness of the dataset (Section 3 Dataset, p. 189)
column7: and (iv) they acknowledge that their logistic regression model achieves only moderate explanatory power (AUC = 0.65), suggesting that other non-actionable factors—such as topic popularity, technology maturity, or expert availability—may not be captured by their framework (Section 5.2 Theoretical implications, p. 198)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly propose multiple directions for future work: (i) they state that future research should investigate additional non-actionable success factors, such as “availability of experts,” “topic popularity,” and “degree of maturity of a technology,” which may further explain question success beyond the current framework (Section 5.2 Theoretical implications, p. 198)
column2: (ii) they explicitly suggest improving affect analysis by adopting sentiment and emotion models better tailored to software engineering text, to reduce misclassification of technical jargon and sarcasm (Section 4.2 Qualitative analysis of emotions, p. 194–195)
column3: and (iii) they explicitly discuss the need for future research and tooling around comment moderation, noting that rudeness and toxicity often appear in comments rather than questions, and suggesting that “Stack Overflow should implement comment moderation sooner rather than later,” which opens avenues for studying moderation mechanisms and their impact on community health (Section 5.1 Discussion – Affect, p. 198)] "

column1: S61,61,Finding and Ranking High-Quality Answers in Community Question Answering Sites,2018,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that “the proposed system is implemented with only one CQA site dataset,” namely Stack Exchange, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other community question answering platforms (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)
column2: they further state that they “used only content-based feature to predict the votes of an answer,” indicating a methodological limitation due to the exclusion of other potentially influential signals such as user interactions and comments (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)
column3: additionally, they note an inherent limitation of machine learning approaches in that they “work with the features that are provided by the users,” which constrains performance to manually engineered features and may miss latent patterns (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly propose several directions for future research: they state that “future work should include the implementation of same methodology with full systems of Stack Exchange (150 topics),” indicating plans to scale the approach across all Stack Exchange sites (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)
column2: they also state that “we can also consider the users’ comments to predict the quality of an answer,” suggesting future integration of comment-based features (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)
column3: they further note that “future research might yield better output with automated feature learning system, deep learning, etc.,” indicating plans to move beyond manual feature engineering (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)
column4: additionally, they state that the model should be tested on other CQA platforms such as “Yahoo! Answers, Quora,” to assess cross-platform applicability (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)
column5: finally, they explicitly suggest that future studies should focus on “behavioural aspects related to adoption and use of such technologies,” recommending the use of technology adoption theories to understand user acceptance of the proposed system (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 65)]
column6: S62,62,FastTagRec: fast tag recommendation for software information sites,2018,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the approach assumes existing tags in software information sites are correct, while in reality “human errors are inevitable,” and issues such as synonymous or inappropriate tags cannot be completely resolved despite filtering rules, which may bias the training data (Section 5.2 Threats to validity, p. 696)
column7: they also explicitly note that the model only utilizes textual descriptions of software objects, as all code snippets and screenshots were removed, limiting the ability of FastTagRec to exploit richer information sources (Section 5.2 Threats to validity, p. 696)
column8: additionally, they acknowledge limitations in generalizability, stating that although FastTagRec is evaluated on one large-scale, three medium-scale, and six small-scale software information sites, more case studies are required to generalize the findings to other sites and kinds of software objects (Section 5.2 Threats to validity, p. 696)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will focus on incorporating code snippets and extracted information from screenshots into the model to extend FastTagRec beyond purely textual content (Section 5.2 Threats to validity, p. 696)
column2: they also explicitly state that more software information sites will be used in future evaluations to further validate and generalize the approach (Section 5.2 Threats to validity, p. 696)]
column3: S63,63,AnswerBot: An Answer Summary Generation Tool Based on Stack Overflow,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that AnswerBot is evaluated only on Java-related questions from Stack Overflow, which limits the generalizability of the results to other programming languages and technical domains (Section 3.1 Data Collection, p. 1135)
column4: they also rely on a relatively small user study involving 8 participants and 100 queries, which may limit the strength and representativeness of the empirical evaluation (Section 4.1 User Study, p. 1136)
column5: additionally, the approach depends on heuristic-based feature design (e.g., semantic patterns, paragraph position, voting signals), which may not capture all aspects of answer quality and diversity across different contexts (Sections 2.2–2.3, pp. 1134–1135)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will enhance AnswerBot to support more programming languages such as C/C++ and Python, extending beyond the current Java-only setting (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 1137)
column2: they also state that they plan to provide more customized search options for users, indicating future work on personalization and configurability of the tool (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 1137)
column3: additionally, they explicitly mention interest in integrating AnswerBot into a broader intelligent Q&A system to further support developers’ problem-solving workflows (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 1137)]
column4: S64,64,Features That Predict the Acceptability of Java and JavaScript Answers on Stack Overflow,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the dataset includes only questions and answers tagged with Java and JavaScript from Stack Overflow between 2014–2016, which “may not represent all the types of questions asked and answers provided” and limits generalizability to other languages, tags, and time periods (Section 7 Threats to Validity, p. 9)
column5: they also explicitly note that Stack Overflow “is not representative of all Q&A portals,” so the identified features may not fully transfer to other platforms, even though similar trends are expected in technology-focused forums (Section 7 Threats to Validity, p. 9)
column6: additionally, they acknowledge that accepted answers are treated as ground truth for answer quality, but “this may not necessarily be the best answer in all cases,” since question askers may lack sufficient expertise, introducing construct validity concerns (Section 7 Threats to Validity, p. 9)
column7: they further note that only two modeling techniques (random forest and neural network) are used, so results may not hold for other models such as SVMs or Bayesian approaches (Section 7 Threats to Validity, p. 9)
column8: finally, they acknowledge that some potentially relevant features (e.g., badges and post history) were excluded based on assumptions, which may affect the completeness of the analysis (Section 7 Threats to Validity, p. 9)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “a plausible next step for future work is to replicate this study for similar Q&A forums,” indicating plans to extend the analysis beyond Stack Overflow to other community question-answering platforms (Section 8 Conclusion and Future Work, p. 9)
