column4: S65,65,How Reliable is the Crowdsourced Knowledge of Security Implementation?,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study focuses on Java security-related code snippets from Stack Overflow and relies on a predefined set of insecurity rules, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other programming languages, security domains, and platforms (Section VII Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 544)
column5: they also explicitly note that manual labeling of code snippets may introduce subjectivity and bias, and that the applied rules may not capture all existing or emerging security vulnerabilities, posing a construct validity limitation (Section VII Threats to Validity – Construct Validity, p. 544)
column6: additionally, they acknowledge that sampling based on clone detection may affect internal validity, as results obtained from sampled snippets may differ from those derived from the full dataset (Section VII Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 544)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will aim to identify more kinds of insecure code by considering additional programming languages and leveraging multiple vulnerability detection tools, as well as developing more automated analysis techniques to reduce reliance on manual inspection and improve detection accuracy (Section VIII Conclusion, p. 545)]
column2: S66,66,Integrating Multi-Level Tag Recommendation with External Knowledge Bases for Automatic Question Answering,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the proposed framework is primarily evaluated on software development–related Q&A sites, particularly Stack Overflow, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other domains
column3: they also note challenges related to identifying appropriate external knowledge bases and scalability when dealing with a much larger number of KBs, implicitly indicating constraints in applying the approach beyond the studied setting (Section 6 Conclusions and Future Work, pp. 20–21)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “an interesting future direction is to apply the proposed framework to other important domains, such as health related ones, or even the ones with open domains,” and further highlight the need to address “the identification of potential KBs and how to better scale the system when a much larger number of KBs are involved,” indicating plans to extend the framework across domains and improve scalability (Section 6 Conclusions and Future Work, pp. 20–21)]
column2: S67,67,SOTagRec: A Combined Tag Recommendation Approach for Stack Overflow,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the proposed SOTagRec approach only uses the textual content of questions and does not consider correlations between tags, which may limit recommendation accuracy (Section 5 Conclusion and Future Work, p. 151)
column3: they also implicitly limit generalizability by evaluating the approach solely on Stack Overflow data with rare tags (appearing fewer than 50 times) filtered out, which may exclude long-tail or emerging tags (Section 4.1 Dataset, p. 148)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “in the future, we plan to utilize the relationship between tags, code snippets and user information to make our tag recommendation more accurate,” indicating plans to incorporate tag correlations, code information, and user-related features to extend and improve SOTagRec (Section 5 Conclusion and Future Work, p. 151)]

S68,68,Towards an Accurate Prediction of the Question Quality on Stack Overflow using a Deep-Learning-Based NLP Approach,2019,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the proposed approach relies solely on linguistic and semantic features extracted from question text (title, body, and tags), and does not incorporate popularity-based or user-related features, which prior work shows can outperform text-only models in predicting question quality (Section 4 Results and Discussion, pp. 636–637)
column2: they also explicitly note limitations of quality labels based on accepted answers and scores, stating that “scores do not always reflect the real quality” and that answer acceptance depends on the question asker, which may introduce noise and construct validity issues (Section 4 Results and Discussion, p. 637)
column3: additionally, the study is conducted only on Stack Overflow data from a specific time period, which may limit generalizability to other platforms or evolving community practices (Section 3 Process Description, pp. 634–635)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will explore improving the Doc2Vec representation through more careful preprocessing techniques, integrating LSTM networks to better capture sequential information in text, and extending the model to predict the actual closing reason of a question using a multi-class classifier, indicating planned methodological and modeling extensions (Section 6 Concluding Remarks, p. 638)]
column2: S69,69,Detecting Duplicate Questions in Stack Overflow via Deep Learning Approaches,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the evaluation is conducted only on Stack Overflow questions from six programming languages (Java, HTML, Python, C++, Ruby, and Objective-C), which “may not guarantee the generalization of our approach,” limiting external validity to other languages and community question answering platforms (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 511)
column3: they also note that recall-rate@k is the sole evaluation metric used, which, although standard, may not capture all aspects of duplicate question detection effectiveness (Section V Threats to Validity – Construct Validity, p. 511)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “in the future, our approach will apply our approach to other Community Question Answering (CQA) websites,” indicating plans to extend and validate the proposed deep learning–based duplicate question detection approaches beyond Stack Overflow to improve generalizability (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 511)]
column2: S70,70,Predicting the Timing and Quality of Responses in Online Discussion Forums,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the empirical evaluation is conducted only on Stack Overflow data, specifically questions tagged with Python over a 30-day period, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other domains, programming languages, time spans, or online discussion forums (Section III Dataset and Descriptive Analytics, p. 1935
column3: Section V Discussion, p. 1939)
column4: they also implicitly note that the extreme sparsity of user–question interactions (only about 0.03% positive cases) poses challenges for prediction and necessitates simplifying modeling choices, which may limit applicability in settings with different participation dynamics (Section III-A Data Collection and Processing, p. 1935)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that “the main next step for future work is incorporating our recommendation system into an online forum platform to observe its impact,” and suggest evaluating the approach via A/B testing to compare response quality and timing with and without the system, indicating future work on real-world deployment and empirical validation of the proposed recommendation framework (Section VI Conclusion, p. 1940)]
column2: S71,71,An empirical study of obsolete answers on stack overflow,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study focuses only on Stack Overflow, which “is one of the most popular and largest Q&A websites,” and therefore the findings may not generalize to other Q&A platforms or knowledge-sharing communities (Section 6 Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 859)
column3: they also explicitly note limitations in their heuristic-based data collection approach, stating that identifying obsolete answers via keyword-based comment analysis achieves only 75 percent accuracy, which introduces noise and potential false positives into the dataset (Section 6 Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 859)
column4: additionally, they acknowledge that their quantitative analysis in RQ1 provides only upper-bound estimates for answer updates and new answers, since edits or new answers may occur for reasons other than obsolescence, posing a threat to internal validity (Section 6 Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, pp. 859–860)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should develop more advanced and accurate approaches to detect obsolete knowledge on Stack Overflow, for example by applying machine learning techniques to identify obsolescence-indicating comments based on semantic meaning rather than simple keyword matching (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 860)
column2: they also explicitly propose future research on extracting and summarizing useful information from comments, so that answer seekers can more easily identify updated or corrective information in long and unstructured comment threads (Section 8 Conclusion, p. 860)]
column3: S72,72,Quality assessment of answers with user-identified criteria and data-driven features in social Q&A,2019,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that Stack Exchange is only one type of social Q&A platform, and therefore the findings “may not represent all other social Q&A sites,” limiting the generalizability of the results beyond the Stack Exchange network (Section 9 Limitations and future research, p. 25)
column4: they also explicitly note that the four selected sites (Mathematics, Ask Ubuntu, Photography, and Arqade) may not fully represent all sites within each knowledge domain, introducing a sampling limitation (Section 9 Limitations and future research, p. 25)
column5: additionally, they acknowledge that their analysis assumes user comments on high-quality answers reflect the criteria users apply when selecting answers, but comments may not capture all criteria users actually consider, posing a construct validity limitation (Section 9 Limitations and future research, p. 25)
column6: they further note that users’ voting behaviors may be biased by factors such as answer position (position bias), meaning high-quality answers may be overlooked if they appear later, which affects the validity of using votes as a proxy for quality (Section 9 Limitations and future research, p. 25)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should involve interviewing users to better understand how they apply different criteria when selecting answers across knowledge domains, addressing limitations of comment-based inference (Section 9 Limitations and future research, p. 25)
column2: they also explicitly propose future experimental and interview-based studies to examine the gap between users’ perceived quality criteria and their actual voting behaviors, as well as the causes of biases such as position effects in answer evaluation (Section 9 Limitations and future research, p. 25)]

column1: S73,73,Assessing the quality of answers autonomously in community question-answering,2019,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the proposed framework can only distinguish between high-quality and low-quality answers and does not provide guidance on how to improve low-quality answers, limiting its practical usefulness for supporting answer refinement (Section 6 Discussion, p. 365)
column2: they also explicitly note that the approach is heavily dependent on communities’ past interactions and user history, which limits its applicability to newly formed or sparsely populated CQA communities where such historical data is unavailable (Section 6 Discussion, p. 365)
column3: additionally, they acknowledge that the framework does not assess the factual correctness of answers, as checking correctness is beyond the scope of the study, which limits the notion of “quality” to community-driven signals rather than objective accuracy (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 366)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should focus on expanding the framework to identify what constitutes low-quality answers in more detail, so as to inform users on how to develop and share better answers in educational and focused CQAs (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 366)
column2: they also explicitly propose integrating automatic methods for detecting the factual correctness of answers into the framework, which would enable more comprehensive quality assessment (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 366)
column3: additionally, they state that future research should investigate how to assist users who struggle to contribute high-quality content and how to support users in creating better answers to improve overall CQA quality and online learning effectiveness (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 366)]
column4: S74,74,Code Duplication on Stack Overflow,2020,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that their study presents only a preliminary investigation and is limited to exact code clones, noting that “extending the analysis to also cover other types of clones will be a logical next step,” which limits the completeness of the current findings (Section 4 Preliminary Analysis, p. 14)
column5: they also explicitly note limitations related to Stack Overflow as a platform, such as the lack of proper code versioning, bug tracking, and bulk edit support, which makes it difficult to consistently maintain duplicated code snippets and may influence the observed duplication patterns (Section 1 Introduction, pp. 13–14)
column6: additionally, the study focuses exclusively on Stack Overflow, which limits the generalizability of the observations to other online forums or code-hosting platforms (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 16)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should investigate additional types of code clones (e.g., non-exact clones) on Stack Overflow, extend the analysis to better understand user behavior such as self-plagiarism and incentives for cloning successful answers, and develop tool support to automatically detect, link, and manage duplicated code snippets, including adding missing attribution and keeping snippets up to date with authoritative sources (Sections 3 Research Questions and 5 Conclusion, pp. 14–16)]
column2: S75,75,Generating Question Titles for Stack Overflow from Mined Code Snippets,2020,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the generated question titles are “still not perfect,” and that the proposed approach suffers from semantic drift in some cases, meaning the generated titles may deviate from the intended semantics of the code snippet (Section 7 Discussion, p. 33)
column3: they also explicitly note that their approach does not incorporate structural information of source code, such as abstract syntax trees (ASTs), because many Stack Overflow code snippets are not parsable, which limits the model’s ability to leverage richer code semantics (Section 7 Discussion, p. 33)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will focus on incorporating more contextual information to reduce semantic drift and generate better question titles (Section 7 Discussion, p. 33)
column2: they also explicitly propose using program repair techniques to fix unparsable code snippets and then exploit structural code information (e.g., ASTs) to further improve title generation quality (Section 7 Discussion, p. 33)]
column3: S76,76,Large-Scale Question Tagging via Joint Question-Topic Embedding Learning,2020,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that their proposed model and evaluation are conducted on a single large-scale collaborative CQA platform (Zhihu), which limits the generalizability of the findings to other CQA systems with different tagging practices or without explicit hierarchical topic structures (Section 1 Introduction
column4: Section 5 Experiments, pp. 20–21)
column5: they also explicitly note that the effectiveness of the approach depends on the availability and quality of a predefined topic hierarchy (DAG), which may not exist or may be incomplete in other platforms such as Stack Overflow or Quora, posing a practical applicability limitation (Section 1 Introduction

column7: additionally, the authors acknowledge that extremely sparse or rare topic tags remain challenging despite the proposed hierarchy-based knowledge transfer, indicating residual limitations in handling severe label imbalance (Section 5.5 On the DAG Structure, pp. 16–17)]

NLP-Based Pre-Submission Predictions of Question Closing Reasons on Stack Overflow,2020,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the multiclass classifier for predicting closing reasons achieves only moderate accuracy, noting that “there is, however, room for further improvement,” and that distinguishing between certain closing reasons (e.g., off-topic vs. unclear) is inherently difficult even for human moderators, which limits prediction performance (Section 4 Results and Discussion, pp. 47–48)
column7: they also explicitly note that the dataset of closing reasons is highly imbalanced, requiring downsampling, which may limit the robustness and realism of the trained models (Section 4 Results and Discussion, p. 47)

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should focus on improving prediction accuracy by incorporating more data and better balancing strategies, including potentially leveraging deleted questions if they become accessible (Section 4 Results and Discussion, p. 48)

column1: S78,78, Duplicate Question Detection With Deep Learning in Stack Overflow,2020,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that their approaches are evaluated only on real Stack Overflow data, which “may not guarantee the generalization of our approaches” to other community question answering websites or domains, indicating a limitation in external validity despite using multiple programming-language datasets (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 25973)

S79,79,Improving Quality of a Post's Set of Answers in Stack Overflow,2020,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study focuses only on Java questions from Stack Overflow, which may limit the generalizability of the proposed SOPI approach to other programming languages and community question answering platforms (Section VI Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 510–511)

column1: S80,80,Predicting closed questions on community question answering sites using convolutional neural network,2020,"Limitations [The authors explicitly note that the study excludes duplicate question prediction, even though duplication is a major reason for question closure on community Q&A sites, which limits the completeness of the proposed model (Section 7 Conclusion and limitations, p. 10569)

column4: S81,81,TagDC: A tag recommendation method for software information sites with a combination of deep learning and collaborative filtering,2020,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that rare tags are removed using a fixed frequency threshold (? 50), following prior work, which may exclude long-tail or emerging tags and limit the ability of TagDC to recommend infrequent but potentially important tags (Section 2.3.1 Data preprocessing, pp. 4–5)

column6: additionally, the approach recommends tags only from a predefined candidate tag set composed of commonly used tags, which limits its ability to suggest novel or newly introduced tags and constrains adaptability to evolving tag vocabularies (Section 2.1 Problem formulation, pp. 3–4)]

Answers (Section 6 Conclusion, Limitations and Future Research Work, p. 820)

column1: S83,83,Understanding stack overflow code quality: A recommendation of caution,2020,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that a large proportion of Stack Overflow code snippets could not be parsed or compiled, noting that only about 33% of the extracted snippets were parsable, which limits the completeness of the quantitative analysis and may bias results toward higher-quality or more structured snippets (Section 6 Threats to Validity, p. ~34)
column2: they also explicitly note that minimal preprocessing (e.g., wrapping snippets in class declarations) introduced artificial violations and constrained the ability to fully assess snippet quality without confounding effects, representing a methodological limitation (Section 6 Threats to Validity, p. ~34)
column3: additionally, the study focuses only on Java code snippets from Stack Overflow, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other programming languages, ecosystems, or Q&A platforms (Section 6 Threats to Validity, p. ~35)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should investigate automated repair and preprocessing techniques to improve the parsability and analyzability of Stack Overflow code snippets, enabling more comprehensive quality assessments (Section 7 Conclusion and Future Work, p. ~36)
column2: they also explicitly propose extending the analysis to other programming languages and platforms to better understand whether the observed quality patterns generalize beyond Java and Stack Overflow (Section 7 Conclusion and Future Work, p. ~36)]

column1: S84,84,Toxic code snippets on stack overflow,2021,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study focuses only on Java code snippets from Stack Overflow and their clones in a curated set of 111 Java open-source projects (Qualitas corpus), which limits the generalizability of the findings to other programming languages, ecosystems, and Q&A platforms (Section 6 Threats to Validity, pp. 573–574)
column2: they also explicitly note that only two types of toxic code snippets—outdated code and license-violating code—are investigated, while other forms such as security vulnerabilities and code smells are excluded, limiting the scope of toxicity analysis (Section 1 Introduction
column3: Section 6 Threats to Validity, pp. 561–562, 573)
column4: additionally, the authors acknowledge that clone detection results depend on tool configuration and threshold choices, and despite careful validation, some false negatives and false positives may still exist, posing internal validity limitations (Section 6 Threats to Validity, p. 574)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should investigate legal problems arising from reusing Stack Overflow code snippets in practice, as their survey revealed that some developers have already faced legal issues, but these could not be analyzed further due to anonymity (Section 3.1.2 Visitor Survey, p. 571)
column2: they also explicitly propose extending the analysis to Stack Overflow answerers with lower reputation to compare awareness and behavior regarding toxic code snippets across experience levels (Section 3.1.1 Answerer Survey, p. 570)

column4: additionally, they acknowledge that their analysis focuses on comments associated with answers and does not consider comments on questions, which may also contain useful information for developers (Section 7 Threats to Validity, p. 27)]

column2: Section 6 Threats to Validity, p. 10)

column1: S87,87,Quantifying Human-Perceived Answer Utility in Non-Factoid Question Answering,2021,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the editorial study involves only 12 participants, which limits the scale and statistical generalizability of the findings, despite enabling more controlled and in-depth assessments (Section 3 Editorial Study, p. 75–76)

column1: S89,89,Contextual Profiling of Stack Overflow Java Code Security Vulnerabilities Initial Insights from a Pilot Study,2021,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study is a pilot study based on an inductive analysis, which introduces subjectivity despite mitigation through tool-based detection and careful manual inspection of Stack Overflow artifacts (Section VII Limitations, p. 1066)

column3: S90,90,Detecting Duplicate Questions in Stack Overflow via Semantic and Relevance Approaches,2021,Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge potential internal validity threats due to possible experimental errors, noting that although the code and experiments were carefully checked, “there may still be potential errors,” especially when reproducing baseline models whose source code was unavailable and had to be reimplemented based on the paper descriptions (Section V Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 118)
column4: they also explicitly note that the study is evaluated on a dataset constructed from Stack Overflow questions in six programming languages, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other domains or Q&A platforms despite additional experiments on Quora (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 118)
column5: additionally, they acknowledge a construct validity concern in that duplicate question detection is modeled as a classification problem using F1-score and accuracy, whereas prior work often treats it as a retrieval problem, which may affect comparability with other studies (Section V Threats to Validity – Construct Validity, p. 118)]

column1: S91,91,Rollback Edit Inconsistencies in Developer Forum,2021,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study focuses exclusively on Stack Overflow, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other developer forums or non-technical collaborative editing platforms (Section VI Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 10)

column3: additionally, they acknowledge construct validity limitations related to identifying rollback inconsistencies solely from body edit histories in the Stack Exchange data dump, which may not capture all contextual reasons behind rollback actions (Section VI Threats to Validity – Construct Validity, p. 10)]

column1: S92,92,Characterization and Prediction of Questions without Accepted Answers on Stack Overflow,2021,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that accepted answers are used as a proxy for correctness, but an accepted answer “may not be helpful anymore due to getting outdated,” and conversely, an unaccepted answer may still be correct or useful for third-party users, which could not be detected in this study (Section VII Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 68)
column2: they also explicitly note that closed questions (e.g., vague, duplicate, off-topic) are included in the dataset, which may affect results, but were not filtered because such questions may receive accepted answers before closure and constitute less than 4% of the dataset (Section VII Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 68)
column3: additionally, the authors explicitly state that although the study analyzes a very large dataset from Stack Overflow, the results “are not generalizable to other CQAs,” limiting external validity beyond Stack Overflow (Section VII Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 68)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will explore other types of external knowledge sources for verification beyond the current information repositories, and design new verification structures to capture richer interaction information for trustworthiness modeling (Section 5 Conclusion, p. 569)]

S94,94,Recency and quality-based ranking question in CQAs: A Stack Overflow case study,2021,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the proposed approach and empirical evaluation are conducted only on Stack Overflow data, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other CQA platforms with different community dynamics, content characteristics, and ranking mechanisms (Section 7 Conclusions, p. 17)
column2: they also explicitly note that the ground truth for recency is created through expert judgment on a relatively small subset of questions and answers, which may introduce subjectivity and limit scalability despite careful annotation procedures (Section 6.1 Dataset and ground truth creation, pp. 9–10)
column3: additionally, they acknowledge that the effectiveness of recency features depends on the presence of explicit temporal signals (e.g., software versions, dates, links) in answers, and questions without such signals cannot be effectively ranked by recency, which constrains applicability (Section 6.1 Dataset and ground truth creation, p. 10)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should explore applying the proposed quality- and recency-based ranking approach to other CQA platforms and domains beyond Stack Overflow to assess robustness and generalizability (Section 7 Conclusions, p. 17)
column2: they also explicitly propose investigating additional or alternative recency indicators and feature engineering strategies to better capture content freshness in cases where explicit temporal cues are missing (Section 7 Conclusions, p. 17)]

column1: S95,95,How to Formulate Specific How-to Questions in Software Development?,2022,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the task knowledge graph (task KG) construction relies on manually defined linguistic patterns for initial seed task extraction, which may limit coverage and introduce bias toward certain question formulations (Section 4.2 Initial Seed Task Extraction, p. 309)

column3: additionally, the task KG and evaluation are built solely from Stack Overflow questions, which limits the generalizability of the approach to other programming-related Q&A systems or forums with different characteristics (Section 8 Discussion and Threats to Validity, p. 325)]

column1: S96,96,PTM4Tag: Sharpening Tag Recommendation of Stack Overflow Posts with Pre-Trained Models,2022,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that Stack Overflow tags are assumed to be correctly labeled by users, but some tags may be mislabeled despite mitigation through discarding rare tags and posts, which introduces potential noise in the ground truth (Section 6.2 Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 9)

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that extensive exploration of alternative strategies for handling long text inputs-such as tail-only truncation, head+tail truncation, compression, or hierarchical methods—is left for future work, as there is no clear conclusion on which strategy performs best across situations (Section 3.1.2 PTM-Oriented Tokenization, p. 4)

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will focus on improving the rejection reason classifier to better identify complex cases with multiple rejection reasons, such as incorrect text or code changes, where current performance is limited (Section 7 Discussion)
column2: they also explicitly propose enhancing EditEx with additional NLP-based features (e.g., spelling and sentence complexity analysis) and richer feedback mechanisms to further assist users during editing (Section 4.5.3 Recommendations for EditEx Improvements)
column3: additionally, they indicate future work on extending EditEx to support broader editing scenarios and integrating it more tightly with the Stack Overflow editing workflow (Section 7 Discussion and Conclusion)]

column1: S118,118,Automated Question Title Reformulation by Mining Modification Logs From Stack Overflow,2023,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the constructed dataset is derived only from Stack Overflow modification logs and focuses on six popular programming languages, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other programming languages, domains, or Q&A platforms (Section VII Discussion on Generalization and Limitations, pp. 4407–4408)
column2: they also note that the dataset is built using heuristic filtering rules (e.g., minimum score and accepted answers), which may bias the data toward high-quality posts and exclude other useful but noisier title reformulation cases (Section II Data Collection
column3: Section VII Discussion, pp. 4392–4393, 4407)
column4: additionally, the approach relies on historical human edits as ground truth, and thus inherits potential biases or suboptimal reformulation choices made by editors in practice (Section VII Discussion on Generalization and Limitations, p. 4408)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will extend the title reformulation task to more programming languages and broader datasets beyond the six studied languages to further validate robustness and generalizability (Section IX Conclusion, p. 4410)
column2: they also explicitly propose improving QETRA by incorporating richer contextual information and more advanced modeling techniques to better handle complex or large-scale title reformulation cases (Section IX Conclusion, p. 4410)
column3: additionally, they indicate future work on enhancing and deploying the browser plugin to better support developers during real-world question editing and title refinement (Section IX Conclusion, p. 4410)]

column1: S119,119,Is Stack Overflow Obsolete? An Empirical Study of the Characteristics of ChatGPT Answers to Stack Overflow Questions,2024,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study analyzes ChatGPT answers generated using the free version of ChatGPT (GPT-3.5), and therefore the findings may not generalize to newer or more capable models (e.g., GPT-4 or later versions) whose behavior and answer quality may differ (Section 9 Limitations, p. 15)
column2: they also explicitly note that the user study involves only 12 participants, which limits the statistical power and generalizability of conclusions about programmer preferences and perceptions (Section 9 Limitations, p. 15)
column3: additionally, the analysis is limited to 517 sampled Stack Overflow questions and focuses on a fixed set of quality dimensions (correctness, consistency, comprehensiveness, conciseness), which may not capture all aspects of answer usefulness or represent the full diversity of programming questions (Section 9 Limitations, p. 15)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work should explore designing interactive and computational methods to detect, highlight, and mitigate misinformation in ChatGPT answers to programming questions, particularly in AI-assisted programming workflows (Section 8 Discussion and Future Work, pp. 13–14)
column2: they also explicitly propose developing tool support to help users verify correctness, such as linking generated answers to official documentation or enabling in-situ code execution and validation (Section 8 Discussion, pp. 13–14)
column3: additionally, they indicate future research directions involving broader evaluations across models, domains, and user populations to better understand the long-term impact of ChatGPT on programmer help-seeking behavior and software quality (Section 8 Discussion and Future Work, pp. 14–15)]

column1: S120,120,Identifying Smart Contract Security Issues in Code Snippets from Stack Overflow,2024,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that SOChecker relies on tag-based filtering (e.g., “Solidity”, “Ethereum”, “ERC20”, “ERC721”) to collect Stack Overflow posts, which may miss relevant smart-contract-related discussions that are not properly tagged, potentially affecting coverage (Section 6 Threats to Validity – Internal Threats, p. 11)
column2: they also explicitly note that they do not directly trace whether vulnerable Stack Overflow code snippets are actually deployed on the Ethereum blockchain due to the impracticality of analyzing the vast amount of on-chain data, which limits conclusions about real-world exploitation (Section 6 Threats to Validity – External Threats, p. 11)
column3: additionally, the approach depends on LLM-based code completion, and incorrect or semantically altered completions may patch or modify original vulnerabilities, leading to false negatives that pruning cannot fully address (Section 5.3 RQ3 Discussion, pp. 10–11)]

column1: Future Work [N/A]

S121,121,Can We Identify Stack Overflow Questions Requiring Code Snippets? Investigating the Cause & Effect of Missing Code Snippets,2024,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the study is conducted only on Stack Overflow data, and therefore “we cannot guarantee the same findings for other Q&A sites,” which limits the generalizability of the results beyond Stack Overflow (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 9)
column2: they also explicitly note that the identification of questions requiring code snippets relies on a manually curated set of seventeen key phrases (e.g., “add code,” “show your code”), which may not capture all relevant cases and can introduce false positives or false negatives despite subsequent manual validation (Section V Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 9)
column3: additionally, the study involves substantial manual analysis and annotation by a small number of annotators, which, although supported by very high inter-rater agreement (? = 0.99), still introduces potential subjectivity and construct validity concerns (Section III-A Dataset Construction
column4: Section V Threats to Validity, pp. 4–5, 9)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that their findings motivate the development of automated support to help users decide, at question submission time, whether their questions require code snippets, suggesting future work on building practical tool support or IDE/Stack Overflow–integrated assistants based on the proposed prediction models (Section I Introduction
column2: Summary of RQ2 and RQ3, pp. 2, 9)
column3: they also implicitly suggest extending the approach to other Q&A platforms and broader contexts to validate whether the observed effects of missing code snippets and the proposed prediction techniques generalize beyond Stack Overflow (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 9)]

column1: S122,122,AUTOGENICS: Automated Generation of Context-Aware Inline Comments for Code Snippets on Programming Q&A Sites Using LLM,2024,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that LLM-generated inline comments are less effective for shorter code snippets and may introduce noisy or unnecessary comments, particularly for trivial statements such as imports or print statements, which reduces overall usefulness without additional filtering (Section X Conclusion
column2: Section VIII Threats to Validity, pp. 32–33)
column3: they also explicitly note that the study focuses only on two programming languages (Java and Python), which limits the generalizability of the findings to other languages with different syntax and commenting conventions (Section VIII Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 32)
column4: additionally, the evaluation relies on manual assessment and a relatively small user study with 14 participants, which, despite high agreement, may still introduce subjectivity and limit broader generalization (Section VIII Threats to Validity – Internal and Statistical Conclusion Validity, pp. 32–33)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will explore evaluating the effectiveness of AUTOGENICS across additional programming languages and other Q&A platforms beyond Stack Overflow to assess broader applicability (Section X Conclusion, p. 33)
column2: they also explicitly plan to conduct expert surveys and collect richer user feedback to further refine AUTOGENICS and improve its usability and practical integration based on practitioner needs (Section X Conclusion, p. 33)]

column1: S123,123,"Reproducibility of issues reported in stack overflow questions: Challenges, impact & estimation",2024,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the reproducibility analysis is largely grounded in Stack Overflow questions related mainly to Java and a smaller validation set of C#, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other programming languages, ecosystems, or non–object-oriented paradigms (Section 5 Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 15)
column2: they also explicitly note that the manual assessment of issue reproducibility and challenge identification involves human judgment, which can introduce subjectivity despite using multiple evaluators and prior validated challenge catalogs (Section 5 Threats to Validity – Construct Validity, p. 15)
column3: additionally, the machine learning models are trained on a relatively modest-sized labeled dataset (357 questions), which may constrain model robustness and scalability when applied to much larger or more diverse real-world datasets (Section 5 Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 15)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work includes developing practical tool support (e.g., browser or IDE plugins) that leverage the proposed reproducibility predictors to assist question askers in improving code snippets before submission (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 16)
column2: they also explicitly propose extending the reproducibility prediction framework to additional programming languages and larger datasets to further validate generalizability and effectiveness (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 16)
column3: additionally, they indicate interest in integrating more advanced features and learning techniques to better capture complex reproducibility challenges beyond static code characteristics (Section 6 Conclusion, p. 16)]

column1: S124,124,PTM4Tag+: Tag recommendation of stack overflow posts with pre-trained models,2024,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that PTM4Tag+ relies on transformer-based pre-trained models with a fixed maximum input length (512 subtokens), requiring truncation strategies (e.g., head-only or tail-only truncation), which may discard potentially useful information from long Stack Overflow posts and thus affect tag recommendation performance (Section 3.1.2 Tokenization
column2: Section 5 Results and Analysis, pp. 11, 20)
column3: they also explicitly note that the framework involves three PTMs (for title, description, and code), which increases model size and inference latency, making deployment more challenging despite experiments with smaller PTMs (Section 1 Introduction
column4: Section 4.4 Implementation
column5: Section 5 Results, pp. 3–4, 16)
column6: additionally, the study is conducted solely on Stack Overflow data, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other software information sites or tagging scenarios with different tag vocabularies and user behaviors (Section 6 Threats to Validity, p. 28)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will investigate leveraging Seq2Seq pre-trained models more broadly as baseline representations for Stack Overflow posts, encouraging further exploration of encoder–decoder PTMs in multi-label classification tasks (Section 1 Introduction
column2: Section 5 Results and Analysis, pp. 4, 18)
column3: they also explicitly propose further optimizing PTM4Tag+ to reduce inference latency and improve deployability, including experimenting with more compact or efficient pre-trained models (Section 1 Introduction
column4: Section 5 Results, pp. 4, 16)
column5: additionally, they indicate future work on extending and validating the framework on other datasets and software information platforms beyond Stack Overflow to assess robustness and generalizability (Section 6 Threats to Validity
column6: Section 8 Conclusion and Future Work, pp. 28, 41)]

column1: S125,125,Towards Better Answers: Automated Stack Overflow Post Updating,2025,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the constructed high-quality dataset still contains noise, as SOUPp achieves 80.8% precision, implying that approximately 19.2% of the automatically identified valid comment–edit pairs may be incorrect, which can negatively affect downstream model training (Section IV-C.1 Automatic Post Updating, p. 6)
column2: they also explicitly note that the study focuses exclusively on Java Stack Overflow posts and single-code-block edits, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other programming languages, multi-block edits, and broader software development contexts (Section IV-B.1 Full-scale dataset preparation
column3: Section VIII Threats to Validity – External Validity, pp. 4, 11)
column4: additionally, the authors explicitly acknowledge that the proposed APU model struggles with complex comments that imply major functional changes or lack concrete editing guidance, which limits its effectiveness for weakness-type or highly abstract comments (Section V-B.4 Manual Analysis, pp. 8–9)
column5: they also note that Exact Match is a conservative metric that underestimates true model capability, since semantically equivalent but syntactically different code updates are counted as errors (Section VIII Threats to Validity – Construct Validity, p. 11)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will focus on designing automated methods that do not rely on manual annotations or handcrafted rules to further improve valid comment–edit prediction precision and reduce noise in the constructed dataset (Section IV-C.1 Automatic Post Updating, p. 6)
column2: they also explicitly propose extending the SOUP framework to other programming languages and platforms beyond Java Stack Overflow posts to improve generalizability (Section VIII Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 11)
column3: additionally, they indicate future research directions on better integrating large language models with Stack Overflow knowledge to handle more complex update scenarios and further enhance real-world applicability (Section VI Discussion – LLMs versus Stack Overflow, p. 11)]

column1: S126,126,Does Editing Improve Answer Quality on Stack Overflow? A Data-Driven  Investigation,2025,"Limitations [The authors explicitly note that the study focuses only on Python-related Stack Overflow answers, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other programming languages and ecosystems (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 10)
column2: they also acknowledge that behavioral similarity between the initial and edited versions of code is not explicitly evaluated, meaning some observed changes in complexity or optimization may stem from semantic differences that are difficult to measure automatically (Section V Threats to Validity – Construct Validity, p. 10)
column3: additionally, the authors mention that code extraction based on HTML tags may occasionally include non-code content (e.g., stack traces), which could affect parsability, executability, and readability analyses, although this impact was found to be minimal based on manual inspection (Section V Threats to Validity – Internal Validity, p. 10)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work includes introducing an automated tool to assess answer revisions in real time by detecting changes in code complexity, vulnerabilities, and readability, with the goal of supporting editors and moderators during the editing process (Section VII Conclusion and Future Work, p. 11)
column2: they also implicitly suggest extending the analysis to other programming languages and broader datasets to further validate and generalize the observed effects of edits on answer quality (Section V Threats to Validity – External Validity
column3: Section VII Conclusion and Future Work, pp. 10–11)]

column1: S127,127,Detecting question relatedness in programming Q&A communities via bimodal feature fusion,2025,"Limitations [The authors explicitly acknowledge that the proposed bimodal approach may still struggle with question pairs exhibiting subtle or complex semantic relatedness, even when both text and code information are available, as illustrated in the case study where both unimodal and bimodal models failed to correctly classify directly related questions (Section 6.1 Discussion, pp. 23–24)
column2: they also note that the effectiveness of the approach is constrained by the limited input sequence length of pre-trained language models and by variability in the quantity and quality of code snippets in question posts, which can affect how representative the extracted code features are (Section 6.1 Discussion, p. 24)
column3: additionally, the authors acknowledge potential external validity limitations, as the evaluation is conducted only on Stack Overflow and a single MOOC dataset, which may limit generalizability to other programming Q&A communities or domains (Section 6.2 Threats to Validity – External Validity, p. 25)]

column1: Future Work [The authors explicitly state that future work will focus on refining the fusion of bimodal features and exploring optimized weighting strategies for combining relatedness scores from text and code modalities to better handle complex semantic cases (Section 6.1 Discussion, p. 24)
column2: they also propose investigating methods for selecting the most representative code snippets when multiple or low-quality snippets are present (Section 6.1 Discussion, p. 24)
column3: additionally, they indicate plans to apply the proposed approach to more related tasks in the PCQA domain and to develop automated tools that assist users in finding similar questions (Section 7 Conclusion, p. 25)]
